Introduction to the topic: atonement and the three-part series on sacrifice
Etymology of 'atonement' as a made-up English word for Bible translation
Greek word 'hilasterion' and its Jewish origins; translation issues
Greek usage in context of offerings to spiritual beings
Hebrew root KFR meaning 'to cover'; trilateral roots explained
Subsidiary meanings of KFR: wipe, smear; noun form means cover/lid
KJV mistranslation of 'mercy seat'; correct meaning is 'cover'
Atonement not a technical theological term; ritual context of Yom Kippur
Yom Kippur means 'day of covering'; why it's needed beyond daily offerings
Sin offerings not transactional; Day of Atonement purifies sacred space, not just people
Ritual atones for altar, ark, tabernacle; sin stains the place
Sacred space vs. chaos; sin brings corruption and hostile powers into the camp
Concentric circles of holiness; sanctuary most threatened, needs purification
Ritual details: high priest's sin offering, incense to veil God's presence
God's appearance on this day; purification needed to avoid destruction
Incense cloud covers high priest; first covering
Two goats: lots cast; both must be perfect
Goat for Yahweh: blood used to purify sanctuary
Blood smeared on altar, ark, fixtures to remove taint; death not ritualized
How blood is applied: sprinkling with finger
Goat for Azazel: sins placed on it via confession; not sacrificed
Scapegoat sent outside camp to Azazel; only time sins placed on animal
Scapegoat rendered unclean; later practice of pushing off cliff to prevent return
Azazel as a demonic being; parallel construction with Yahweh; early Jewish sources
Leviticus 17:7 warns against worshiping goat spirits; clarifies ritual not sacrifice to Azazel
Azazel as source of sin; sins sent back to him; later imagery of Baphomet
New Testament references to atonement are about Day of Atonement ritual
Second Temple literature on Azazel: 1 Enoch, 2 Enoch, Apocalypse of Abraham
Azazel as leader of rebellious watchers; taught forbidden knowledge (metallurgy, etc.)
Contrast with Prometheus myth; Bible says that knowledge was bad
Eschatological Day of Atonement in Second Temple literature; final defeat of Azazel
Christ as fulfillment; angel of the Lord (Yahoel) as high priest in Apocalypse of Abraham
John's literature picks up eschatological Day of Atonement; Christ as high priest
1 John 2:2: atonement for whole world; purification of space, not just sins
1 John 3:8: Christ destroys works of devil; connection to Azazel and Cain
Azazel bound and thrown into pit; parallels Revelation's dragon
Hebrews 2:14: Christ destroys devil through death; eschatological Day of Atonement
Matthew 25: sheep and goats; goats associated with sin and Azazel
Christ as scapegoat in Matthew 27: scarlet robe, reed, mocking, spitting
Matthew's narrative theology: Christ fulfills Day of Atonement; not anti-Semitic
His blood be upon us: blessing, not curse; Hebrews 9:18-22 on sprinkling
Christ dies outside the camp (Hebrews 13:11-12); both goats
Doxology: 'take away the sin of the world' (scapegoat) and 'sins of the world' (purification)
Luke-Acts: Christ as goat for Yahweh; purification of sacred space expanded to cosmos
1 John 2:2: atonement for whole world; purification of all creation
Luke 24: Emmaus road; connection to Maccabean rededication of temple
Luke ends with disciples in temple; Acts begins with Holy Spirit; purification leads to indwelling
Peter's vision: unclean animals made clean; Cornelius; mission to Gentiles
Hebrews 9: purification with blood; context is space, not just forgiveness
Hebrews 10: blood of covenant sanctifies; Christ as goat for Yahweh
Christ's ascension as entering heavenly sanctuary; Hebrews 9:11-14
Caller question on 1 John 2:2 translation; 'only' is adverb, not adjective
Correct translation: 'not only for our sins but for the whole world'; refers to space, not people
Consequences of mistranslation: Western theories of sufficiency vs. efficiency
Isaiah 53: suffering servant; Matthew 8:17 interprets as healing ministry
Athanasius: incarnation purifies humanity; Christ sanctifies all stages of human life
Gregory the Theologian: 'what is not assumed is not healed'; atonement as purification through incarnation
John 17:18-19: Christ sanctifies himself to sanctify disciples; high priestly prayer
Avoid mechanical theories of atonement; focus on participation in Christ's purification
Christian life as participation in cosmic realities; Lent and Pascha as purification
Conclusion: experience salvation, not just understand it; ritual participation
